justification: Cosmopolitan identity is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning self-identification as a world citizen, global vs. national identity framing, and interest in other cultures; no single item captures the full construct. p19_1, p20, and p11 are conceptually coherent indicators of outward cosmopolitan orientation and use broadly compatible ordinal scales (4–6 categories), making PCA appropriate to extract the dominant latent dimension. p15 (pride in being Mexican) is excluded because national pride is conceptually orthogonal to cosmopolitanism and would introduce construct-irrelevant variance into the composite.

Transnational exposure is a latent behavioral construct best captured by combining indicators of cross-border social ties and direct international experience rather than any single item. p8, p8_2, p7, and p3 all tap interpersonal transnational contact (having people abroad, communicating with them, relationships with foreigners in Mexico, and having lived abroad), forming a coherent social-exposure dimension. p2 (languages) and p4 (travel frequency) are excluded because p2 has very low entropy (H=0.80) and p4's 15-category scale is incompatible with the others, and both tap different facets (linguistic capital, tourism) that would dilute the social-exposure signal.

p24_1 (frequency of consuming American fast food) has the highest entropy (H=2.18) among the available items and serves as a concrete behavioral indicator of actual engagement with foreign cultural products rather than stated preferences or attitudes. The remaining items use incompatible scales and tap distinct sub-facets (reading habits, music preference, normative evaluation of foreign influence, TV consumption origin) that do not form a unidimensional scale, making aggregation methodologically risky. As a behavioral frequency measure with seven ordered categories and maximum distributional spread, p24_1 provides the most reliable single bridge variable for this construct.

Economic globalization attitudes constitute a coherent attitudinal construct best measured by combining evaluations of trade openness, FTA support, and the bilateral US-Mexico trade relationship, which together span the key dimensions of trade liberalization sentiment. p47, p48, and p31_1 use compatible ordinal scales (4–6 categories) and all tap pro- vs. anti-globalization trade attitudes with reasonable entropy (H=1.39, 1.82, 1.60), making a mean score appropriate after rescaling to a common range. p46_1 is excluded as it closely overlaps with p48 (both assess FTA valence), and p49_1 (foreign investment in telecoms) taps a more specific regulatory attitude that may not load on the same general trade-openness dimension.

International institutional engagement is a multi-faceted construct encompassing preferences for multilateral governance, support for international human rights mechanisms, and willingness to use international bodies, requiring aggregation to capture its breadth. p54_1, p57, p58, and p60_1 all directly address the role and authority of international institutions (UN, regional bodies) across governance, human rights, and security domains, using compatible 4–7 category ordinal scales with moderate-to-good entropy. p52_1 and p63_1 are excluded because they have very low entropy (H=0.91 and H=0.66 respectively), indicating near-ceiling agreement that would compress variance and reduce bridge model discriminability.
